---
title: "Ch 12 — Surface Areas and Volumes (Combined Solids)"
subtitle: "Glue a cone on a hemisphere, slice a frustum off a cone — and add the parts"
lesson-id: "cbse10.surface-areas-and-volumes"
tier: "cbse10"
ncert-class: 10
ncert-chapter: 12
ncert-chapter-title: "Surface Areas and Volumes"
board-exam: true
prerequisites: [cbse9.surface-areas-and-volumes]
unlocks: [cbse11.three-d-geometry, cbse12.application-of-integrals]
applied-domains: [game-dev, research]
estimated-minutes: 50
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you state the volume and surface area of a cube of side $a$? See
   [`cbse9.surface-areas-and-volumes`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/11-surface-areas-and-volumes/index.qmd).
2. Can you state the volume and CSA (curved surface area) of a cylinder of
   radius $r$ and height $h$?
3. Do you know the volume and CSA of a cone of radius $r$, height $h$,
   slant height $\ell = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$?
4. Do you know the volume and surface area of a sphere of radius $r$?

Three yeses, ready.
:::

## Touch

A pencil. The body is a cylinder; the sharpened tip is a cone. Pencil =
cylinder + cone (glued along a flat face).

A capsule (the medicine kind). The middle is a cylinder; the two end caps
are hemispheres. Capsule = cylinder + 2 hemispheres.

```{ojs}
viewof shape = Inputs.select(["pencil (cylinder + cone)", "capsule (cylinder + 2 hemispheres)", "ice-cream (cone + hemisphere)"], {label: "Composite shape"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof rad12 = Inputs.range([1, 5], {value: 2, step: 0.5, label: "Radius r (cm)"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof len12 = Inputs.range([2, 12], {value: 6, step: 1, label: "Cylinder length / cone height (cm)"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const r = rad12, L = len12;
  let report;
  if (shape === "pencil (cylinder + cone)") {
    const Vcyl = Math.PI * r * r * L;
    const Vcone = Math.PI * r * r * L / 3;
    const Vtot = Vcyl + Vcone;
    report = `Pencil volume = πr²L + (1/3)πr²L = ${Vtot.toFixed(2)} cm³`;
  } else if (shape === "capsule (cylinder + 2 hemispheres)") {
    const Vcyl = Math.PI * r * r * L;
    const Vsph = (4 / 3) * Math.PI * r * r * r;
    const Vtot = Vcyl + Vsph;
    report = `Capsule volume = πr²L + (4/3)πr³ = ${Vtot.toFixed(2)} cm³`;
  } else {
    const Vcone = Math.PI * r * r * L / 3;
    const Vhemi = (2 / 3) * Math.PI * r * r * r;
    const Vtot = Vcone + Vhemi;
    report = `Ice-cream volume = (1/3)πr²L + (2/3)πr³ = ${Vtot.toFixed(2)} cm³`;
  }
  return html`<pre style="font-family: ui-monospace, monospace; font-size: 13px;">${report}
r = ${r} cm, L = ${L} cm</pre>`;
}
```

The recipe: identify the parts, compute each volume separately, then add.
The same applies to surface area — but be careful: glued faces *disappear*
from the outside.

## Wonder

1. The pencil has a cylinder volume + a cone volume. Why do we **add**
   them? When does an *and* of two shapes become a **plus**?
2. The capsule's surface is the lateral surface of the cylinder + the two
   hemispheres' surfaces. The two flat circles where they meet — are they
   visible from outside?
3. A frustum (a bucket) is a cone with the tip chopped off. How would you
   compute its volume?
4. If you melt a sphere and re-cast it as a cube, what stays the same and
   what changes?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) Volumes add when shapes don't overlap. (2) The flat join-faces are
hidden — don't include them in surface area. (3) Frustum volume = full
cone volume $-$ tip cone volume. (4) Volume is preserved; surface area
generally is not.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Volume of a cone + hemisphere
A solid is in the shape of a cone of radius $7$ cm and height $24$ cm
mounted on a hemisphere of the same radius. Find the total volume. (Use
$\pi = 22/7$.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Total = cone volume + hemisphere volume.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Cone: $\frac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{22}{7} \cdot 49
\cdot 24 = 22 \cdot 56 = 1232 \text{ cm}^3$.
Hemisphere: $\frac{2}{3} \pi r^3 = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{22}{7} \cdot 343
= \frac{2156}{3} \approx 718.67 \text{ cm}^3$.
Total $\approx \mathbf{1950.67 \text{ cm}^3}$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — Surface area of a capsule
A capsule has a cylindrical part of length $10$ cm and hemispherical ends
of radius $3$ cm. Find the total surface area. (Use $\pi = 22/7$.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Cylinder lateral + 2 hemispheres' curved surfaces — no flat faces.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Cylinder lateral = $2\pi r h = 2 \cdot \frac{22}{7} \cdot 3 \cdot 10 =
\frac{1320}{7} \approx 188.57$.
Two hemispheres' curved = full sphere = $4\pi r^2 = 4 \cdot \frac{22}{7}
\cdot 9 = \frac{792}{7} \approx 113.14$.
Total $\approx \mathbf{301.71 \text{ cm}^2}$.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Frustum volume by subtraction
A cone has height $H$ and base radius $R$. The top portion is sliced off
by a plane parallel to the base, forming a small cone of height $h$ and
top radius $r$ (and leaving a frustum). Argue why the frustum's volume is
$\frac{1}{3} \pi (R^2 H - r^2 h)$, and check the standard formula
$$V_{\text{frustum}} = \frac{1}{3} \pi h_f (R^2 + r^2 + Rr),$$
where $h_f = H - h$ is the frustum's height.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Big cone $-$ small cone gives frustum. Use $r/R = h/H$ (similar triangles)
to relate.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
By similar triangles, $r/R = h/H$, so $h = Hr/R$.
Big cone volume: $\frac{1}{3}\pi R^2 H$. Small cone: $\frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h
= \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 \cdot Hr/R = \frac{1}{3}\pi H r^3/R$.
Frustum volume = $\frac{1}{3}\pi H \cdot \dfrac{R^3 - r^3}{R}$
$= \frac{1}{3}\pi H \cdot \dfrac{(R - r)(R^2 + Rr + r^2)}{R}$.
With $h_f = H - h = H(R - r)/R$:
$V = \frac{1}{3}\pi h_f (R^2 + Rr + r^2)$. $\square$
</details>

### Problem 4 — Melting and recasting
A solid sphere of radius $6$ cm is melted and recast into a cylinder of
radius $4$ cm. Find the height of the cylinder.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Volume is preserved.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\frac{4}{3} \pi (6)^3 = \pi (4)^2 h \Rightarrow \frac{4 \cdot 216}{3} =
16 h \Rightarrow 288 = 16 h \Rightarrow h = 18$ cm.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Hollow shape
A hollow sphere has external radius $4$ cm and internal radius $2$ cm. It
is melted and recast into a cone of base radius $4$ cm. Find the height.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Volume of the hollow shell = $\frac{4}{3}\pi (R^3 - r^3)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Shell volume = $\frac{4}{3}\pi(64 - 8) = \frac{4}{3}\pi \cdot 56 = \frac{224
\pi}{3}$.
Cone volume = $\frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h = \frac{1}{3}\pi \cdot 16 \cdot h$.
Equate: $\frac{224\pi}{3} = \frac{16\pi h}{3} \Rightarrow h = 14$ cm.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Surface area of a frustum
A bucket has top radius $20$ cm, bottom radius $8$ cm, height $16$ cm.
Find its slant height and curved surface area.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Slant height $\ell = \sqrt{h^2 + (R - r)^2}$. CSA = $\pi (R + r) \ell$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\ell = \sqrt{16^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{256 + 144} = \sqrt{400} = 20$ cm.
CSA = $\pi (20 + 8) \cdot 20 = 560 \pi$. With $\pi = 22/7$: $560 \cdot
\frac{22}{7} = 1760 \text{ cm}^2$.
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Standard solid formulas** ($r$ = radius, $h$ = height, $\ell$ = slant
height, $a$ = side):

| Solid | Volume | Curved/Lateral SA | Total SA |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Cube | $a^3$ | $4a^2$ | $6a^2$ |
| Cuboid $l \times b \times h$ | $lbh$ | $2h(l + b)$ | $2(lb + bh + hl)$ |
| Cylinder | $\pi r^2 h$ | $2\pi r h$ | $2\pi r(r + h)$ |
| Cone ($\ell = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$) | $\frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h$ | $\pi r \ell$ | $\pi r(r + \ell)$ |
| Sphere | $\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ | $4\pi r^2$ | $4\pi r^2$ |
| Hemisphere | $\frac{2}{3}\pi r^3$ | $2\pi r^2$ | $3\pi r^2$ |

**Frustum of a cone** (radii $R, r$; height $h_f$; slant $\ell_f = \sqrt{
h_f^2 + (R - r)^2}$):

- Volume: $\frac{1}{3} \pi h_f (R^2 + r^2 + Rr)$.
- Curved SA: $\pi (R + r) \ell_f$.
- Total SA: $\pi(R + r)\ell_f + \pi R^2 + \pi r^2$.

**Combined solids:** add volumes for non-overlapping pieces; for surface
areas, **exclude internal joining faces**. **Melting and recasting** =
volume conserved, surface area generally changes.

:::

A verse:

> *Cone, sphere, cylinder, cube — each one its formula true.*
> *Glue them — add their volumes; the joining face does not show through.*
> *Melt and recast? Volume holds — every drop accounted for.*
> *Frustum's a chopped cone: subtract the tip, that's all you need to score.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A toy is in the form of a cone of radius $3.5$ cm mounted on a hemisphere
> of the same radius. The total height of the toy is $15.5$ cm. Find the
> total surface area of the toy. (Use $\pi = 22/7$.)

The hemisphere has radius $3.5$ cm. The cone sits on it. So cone height =
$15.5 - 3.5 = 12$ cm.

Slant height of cone: $\ell = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{12.25 + 144} =
\sqrt{156.25} = 12.5$ cm.

Total surface area = (cone curved SA) + (hemisphere curved SA). The flat
circle where they meet is hidden.

$= \pi r \ell + 2 \pi r^2$
$= \pi r (\ell + 2r)$
$= \frac{22}{7} \cdot 3.5 \cdot (12.5 + 7)$
$= \frac{22}{7} \cdot 3.5 \cdot 19.5$
$= 11 \cdot 19.5$
$= \mathbf{214.5 \text{ cm}^2}$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Volume of a cylinder, $r = 7$ cm, $h = 10$ cm.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi r^2 h = \frac{22}{7} \cdot 49 \cdot 10 = 1540 \text{ cm}^3$.
</details>

**W2.** A solid hemisphere has radius $7$ cm. Find its total surface area.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3\pi r^2 = 3 \cdot \frac{22}{7} \cdot 49 = 462 \text{ cm}^2$.
</details>

**W3.** A cone has radius $5$, slant height $13$. Find height and CSA.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$h = \sqrt{169 - 25} = 12$. CSA = $\pi r \ell = 65\pi \approx 204.20 \text{
cm}^2$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A solid in the form of a right circular cylinder, with one end a
hemisphere and the other a cone. The common radius is $4$ cm, the height
of the cylinder is $40$ cm, the height of the cone is $3$ cm. Find the
volume.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Volume = cylinder + hemisphere + cone.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Cylinder: $\pi \cdot 16 \cdot 40 = 640\pi$.
Hemisphere: $\frac{2}{3}\pi \cdot 64 = \frac{128\pi}{3}$.
Cone: $\frac{1}{3}\pi \cdot 16 \cdot 3 = 16\pi$.
Total = $640\pi + \frac{128\pi}{3} + 16\pi = \frac{1920\pi + 128\pi + 48\pi}{3}
= \frac{2096\pi}{3} \approx \frac{2096 \cdot 22}{21} = \frac{46112}{21}
\approx 2195.81 \text{ cm}^3$.
</details>

**P2.** A cylindrical container of radius $6$ cm and height $15$ cm is full
of ice cream which is divided among $10$ children in equal cones with
hemispherical tops on top. If the height of the conical portion is $4$
times the radius of its base, find the radius of the ice-cream cone.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Total ice cream volume / 10 = volume per child = cone + hemisphere.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Total ice cream = $\pi \cdot 36 \cdot 15 = 540\pi$. Per child: $54\pi$.
Per child = cone + hemisphere = $\frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 (4r) + \frac{2}{3}\pi
r^3 = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 + \frac{2}{3}\pi r^3 = 2\pi r^3$.
$2\pi r^3 = 54\pi \Rightarrow r^3 = 27 \Rightarrow r = 3$ cm.
</details>

**P3.** A drinking glass is in the shape of a frustum of a cone of height
$14$ cm. The diameters of its two circular ends are $4$ cm and $2$ cm.
Find the capacity (volume) of the glass.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$R = 2, r = 1, h_f = 14$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$V = \frac{1}{3}\pi h_f(R^2 + r^2 + Rr) = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{22}{7}
\cdot 14 \cdot (4 + 1 + 2) = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{22}{7} \cdot 14 \cdot
7 = \frac{1}{3} \cdot 22 \cdot 14 = \frac{308}{3} \approx 102.67 \text{
cm}^3$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** A vessel is in the form of an inverted cone, height $8$ cm and
radius at top $5$ cm. It is filled with water up to the brim. When lead
shots, each spherical with radius $0.5$ cm, are dropped into the vessel,
one-fourth of the water flows out. Find the number of lead shots.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Volume displaced = $\frac{1}{4}$ vessel volume.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Vessel volume = $\frac{1}{3}\pi \cdot 25 \cdot 8 = \frac{200\pi}{3}$.
$\frac{1}{4}$ of this = $\frac{50\pi}{3}$.
Each lead shot = $\frac{4}{3}\pi (0.5)^3 = \frac{4\pi}{3 \cdot 8} =
\frac{\pi}{6}$.
$n = \dfrac{50\pi/3}{\pi/6} = \dfrac{50 \cdot 6}{3} = 100$ shots.
</details>

**S2.** Water is being pumped out through a circular pipe of internal
diameter $7$ cm at the rate of $5$ m/s. Calculate the volume of water (in
litres) discharged in $30$ minutes.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Volume per second = cross-section area × speed. Convert units.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Cross-section area = $\pi (3.5)^2 = \frac{22}{7} \cdot 12.25 = 38.5 \text{
cm}^2 = 0.00385 \text{ m}^2$.
Volume per second = $0.00385 \cdot 5 = 0.01925 \text{ m}^3$ = 19.25 litres.
Per 30 min (1800 s): $19.25 \cdot 1800 = \mathbf{34650}$ litres.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**Q1 (1 mark — MCQ).** The volume of a sphere is $\dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$.
The volume of a hemisphere of the same radius is:
(a) $\dfrac{2}{3}\pi r^3$  (b) $\dfrac{1}{2}\pi r^3$  (c) $\dfrac{4}{6}\pi
r^3$  (d) Both (a) and (c).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{2}{3}\pi r^3 = \dfrac{4}{6}\pi r^3$. **(d).**
</details>

**Q2 (1 mark — MCQ).** A solid is hemispherical at one end and conical at
the other. The radii of both are equal. The slant height of the cone is
$5$ cm and base radius $4$ cm. The total surface area is:
(a) $20\pi$  (b) $52\pi$  (c) $32\pi$  (d) $36\pi$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Cone CSA = $\pi r \ell = 20\pi$. Hemisphere curved = $2\pi r^2 = 32\pi$.
Total = $52\pi$. **(b).**
</details>

**Q3 (2 marks).** A wooden article was made by scooping out a hemisphere
from each end of a solid cylinder. If the height of the cylinder is $20$
cm and its base radius is $3.5$ cm, find the **volume** of the wood
remaining.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Cylinder volume $-$ 2 hemispheres = cylinder $-$ 1 sphere.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$V = \pi r^2 h - \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \frac{22}{7}(12.25 \cdot 20 -
\frac{4}{3} \cdot 42.875) = \frac{22}{7}(245 - 57.17) = \frac{22}{7} \cdot
187.83 \approx 590.33 \text{ cm}^3$.

Cleaner: $\frac{22}{7}(245 - \frac{171.5}{3}) = \frac{22}{7} \cdot
\frac{735 - 171.5}{3} = \frac{22}{7} \cdot \frac{563.5}{3} = \frac{12397}
{21} \approx \mathbf{590.33 \text{ cm}^3}$.
</details>

**Q4 (2 marks).** A solid metallic sphere of radius $6$ cm is melted and
drawn into a wire of diameter $0.4$ cm. Find the length of the wire.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Sphere volume = wire (cylinder) volume.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\frac{4}{3}\pi (6)^3 = \pi (0.2)^2 \cdot L \Rightarrow 288\pi = 0.04\pi L
\Rightarrow L = 7200$ cm = $72$ m.
</details>

**Q5 (3 marks).** A solid is composed of a cylinder with hemispherical
ends. If the whole length of the solid is $108$ cm and the diameter of the
hemispherical ends is $36$ cm, find the cost of polishing its surface at
the rate of $\text{₹}7$ per $1000 \text{ cm}^2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$r = 18$, cylinder height $L = 108 - 2 \cdot 18 = 72$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Cylinder lateral = $2\pi r L = 2 \cdot \frac{22}{7} \cdot 18 \cdot 72 =
\frac{56\pi \cdot 162}{?}$ — recompute cleanly: $2\pi r L = 2 \cdot
\frac{22}{7} \cdot 18 \cdot 72 = \frac{2 \cdot 22 \cdot 18 \cdot 72}{7} =
\frac{57024}{7} \approx 8146.29$.
Two hemispheres = sphere = $4\pi r^2 = 4 \cdot \frac{22}{7} \cdot 324 =
\frac{28512}{7} \approx 4073.14$.
Total surface = $\frac{57024 + 28512}{7} = \frac{85536}{7} \approx
12219.43 \text{ cm}^2$.
Cost = $\frac{12219.43}{1000} \cdot 7 \approx \mathbf{₹85.54}$.
</details>

**Q6 (3 marks).** A bucket open at the top is in the shape of a frustum
of a cone with capacity $12308.8 \text{ cm}^3$. The radii of its top and
bottom are $20$ cm and $12$ cm. Find the height of the bucket.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$V = \frac{1}{3}\pi h(R^2 + r^2 + Rr) = \frac{1}{3}\pi h(400 + 144 + 240)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$12308.8 = \frac{1}{3} \cdot 3.14 \cdot h \cdot 784 \Rightarrow 12308.8 =
820.69 \cdot h \Rightarrow h \approx 15$ cm.
</details>

**Q7 (5 marks — long answer).** A tent is in the shape of a cylinder
surmounted by a conical top. If the height and diameter of the cylindrical
part are $2.1$ m and $4$ m respectively, and the slant height of the top
is $2.8$ m, find the area of the canvas used for making the tent. Also
find the cost of the canvas at the rate of $\text{₹}500$ per $\text{m}^2$.
(Note: the floor of the tent is *not* canvas.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Canvas = cylinder lateral + cone curved.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$r = 2$ m, $h_{\text{cyl}} = 2.1$ m, $\ell = 2.8$ m.
Cylinder lateral = $2\pi r h = 2 \cdot \frac{22}{7} \cdot 2 \cdot 2.1 =
\frac{2 \cdot 22 \cdot 4.2}{7} = \frac{184.8}{7} = 26.4 \text{ m}^2$.
Cone curved = $\pi r \ell = \frac{22}{7} \cdot 2 \cdot 2.8 = \frac{123.2}{7}
= 17.6 \text{ m}^2$.
Total canvas = $44 \text{ m}^2$. Cost = $44 \cdot 500 = \mathbf{₹22000}$.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar 12.3 Q1–Q12 cover combined-solid
volume/SA problems like Q3 and Q7; Exemplar 12.4 Q1–Q12 cover melting-
and-recasting problems like Q4 and S1.

**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE 2020 Set 1, Q35 mirrors Q7. CBSE 2023
Standard, Q33 mirrors Q5.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Surface area of glued solid = sum of all surface areas."** No — the
   *internal* faces (where the parts meet) are hidden. Subtract those, or
   compute only the externally visible parts.
2. **"Melting changes the volume."** It doesn't (assuming no spillage).
   Volume is conserved; that's the whole point of recasting problems.
3. **"Slant height of a cone equals the radius."** No — slant height $\ell
   = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$. It's the hypotenuse of the right triangle formed
   by the height and the base radius.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you list (without notes) the standard volume and SA formulas for
   cube, cuboid, cylinder, cone, sphere, hemisphere?
2. Did Q5 and Q7 fall out cleanly with at most one hint?
3. Could you spot which faces of a glued composite are hidden, before
   computing surface area?

- **3 yes:** move on.
- **2 yes:** redo Q5 and Q7. The board paper picks one of these every
  year.
- **0–1 yes:** the formula list is non-negotiable. Memorise it; the rest
  is bookkeeping.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`cbse9.surface-areas-and-volumes`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/11-surface-areas-and-volumes/index.qmd) —
  base mensuration formulas.

### What this unlocks

- [`cbse11.three-d-geometry`](../../tier-cbse-11-class-11/11-three-d-geometry-intro/index.qmd) —
  generalising 3D to coordinates.
- [`cbse12.application-of-integrals`](../../tier-cbse-12-class-12/08-area-under-curves/index.qmd) —
  re-deriving these volumes via the disc method.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 10, Chapter 12 — Surface Areas and Volumes** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 12.1–12.2: surface area of
  combined solids, volume of combined solids. (Section 12.3 — frustum
  of a cone — was retained but slightly reduced; the formulas remain
  examinable.)
- **Sister synthesis lesson:**
  [`t1.area-perimeter-volume`](../../tier-1-foundations/13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd) —
  basic mensuration from scratch.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Every 3D model in a game is internally a list of
*primitive solids* (boxes, spheres, cylinders, cones, frusta) with
known volumes — used for physics, collision, mass, and inertia
calculations.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** The volume formulas you've been using all come from the
calculus method of *Pappus and Cavalieri* — slicing a solid into
infinitesimally thin layers. The Class 12 integration chapter
re-derives every one of them via $\int_a^b \pi r(x)^2 \, dx$.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** Bhāskara (formula table); Russian school
  (problem-first); Better Explained (the *Touch* pencil/capsule
  metaphors).
- **Source notes:** Q3 and Q7 follow standard NCERT 12.2 / 12.3
  Examples; Q5 mirrors NCERT Example 7.
:::
